name: Semiconductor Electronics: Materials, Devices and Simple Circuits
subject: Physics

sections.heading: 1. Classification of Solids by Conductivity and Bands
sections.body:
By resistivity:

  • Metals: $\rho \sim 10^{-8}$ – $10^{-6}$ Ω·m.
  • Semiconductors: $\rho \sim 10^{-5}$ – $10^{6}$ Ω·m.
  • Insulators: $\rho \sim 10^{11}$ – $10^{19}$ Ω·m.

Band theory: in solids, atomic energy levels broaden into bands. The two highest occupied bands matter most — valence band (mostly filled) and conduction band (mostly empty). The energy gap $E_g$ separates them.

  • Metal: bands overlap or conduction band is partly filled — abundant free carriers; conductive at all temperatures.
  • Insulator: $E_g \gtrsim 3$ eV — too large for thermal excitation; almost no carriers.
  • Semiconductor: $E_g \sim 1$ eV (Si: 1.1 eV, Ge: 0.7 eV) — small enough that thermal excitation creates some electron-hole pairs at room temperature.

sections.heading: 2. Intrinsic Semiconductors
sections.body:
Pure (intrinsic) Si or Ge: each atom has 4 valence electrons forming covalent bonds. At $T = 0$ K, no free carriers. At room temperature, thermal energy breaks some bonds, creating electron-hole pairs: a free electron in the conduction band and a hole (vacancy) in the valence band.

Both carriers contribute to conduction:

  • Electrons drift opposite to $\vec{E}$ (negative charge).
  • Holes drift along $\vec{E}$ (effectively positive charge — neighbouring electron 'hops' into vacancy, hole moves opposite).

For intrinsic semiconductor: $n_e = n_h = n_i$. Concentration $n_i$ rises rapidly with temperature, so resistivity falls (negative temperature coefficient).

sections.heading: 3. Extrinsic Semiconductors — Doping
sections.body:
Doping: adding small amounts (1 in $10^6$ to $10^9$) of pentavalent or trivalent impurity to drastically increase conductivity.

n-type: doped with pentavalent atoms (P, As, Sb). Four electrons form covalent bonds; the fifth is loosely bound, easily becomes free electron. Donor level lies just below conduction band.

  • Majority carriers: electrons. Minority: holes.
  • $n_e \gg n_h$, but $n_e n_h = n_i^2$ (mass-action law).

p-type: doped with trivalent (B, Al, In, Ga). Three electrons form bonds; one bond has a hole. Acceptor level lies just above valence band.

  • Majority: holes. Minority: electrons.
  • $n_h \gg n_e$.

The crystal as a whole remains electrically neutral.

sections.heading: 4. p-n Junction
sections.body:
Formed by joining a p-type and n-type semiconductor in a single crystal. Two phenomena occur:

  • Diffusion: electrons diffuse n→p, holes diffuse p→n (concentration gradients). Leaves immobile positive donor ions on n-side and negative acceptor ions on p-side near the junction → depletion region.
  • The exposed ion charges create an internal electric field (n→p), which drives a drift current opposite to diffusion.

At equilibrium, drift balances diffusion. The depletion region (~$10^{-6}$ m wide) has no mobile carriers and a built-in potential barrier $V_0$ (~0.7 V for Si, 0.3 V for Ge).

sections.heading: 5. p-n Junction Diode under Forward and Reverse Bias
sections.body:
Forward bias: p-side connected to + terminal, n-side to − terminal of battery. External field opposes built-in field, reducing barrier and depletion width. Once external $V > V_0$, large current flows (mostly diffusion of majority carriers across junction). Small forward voltage produces large current — exponential I-V relation.

Reverse bias: p-side to −, n-side to +. External field aids built-in field, widens depletion region and increases barrier. Only a tiny reverse saturation current flows (due to thermally generated minority carriers crossing the junction). Independent of voltage (saturation) until breakdown voltage, where current abruptly rises (Zener / avalanche).

Diode is essentially a one-way valve. I-V curve highly non-linear.

sections.heading: 6. Diode as Rectifier
sections.body:
A diode converts AC to DC: passes current in one direction only.

Half-wave rectifier: single diode in series with load. Conducts only during one half of AC cycle; output is pulsating DC, frequency = input frequency. Low efficiency; large ripple.

Full-wave rectifier: two diodes with a centre-tapped transformer (or four diodes in a bridge). Both halves of AC are utilised; output frequency = 2× input. Higher efficiency, smaller ripple. With a smoothing capacitor (filter) across the load, output approaches steady DC.

Used in every battery charger, AC adapter, and DC power supply.

sections.heading: 7. Special-Purpose Diodes
sections.body:
Zener diode: heavily doped p-n junction designed to operate in reverse breakdown without damage. Beyond Zener voltage $V_Z$, voltage stays constant despite changing current — used as voltage regulator.

Photodiode: reverse-biased p-n junction in transparent package; incident photons (with $h\nu > E_g$) generate electron-hole pairs, increasing reverse current proportional to light intensity. Used in light sensors, optical communication.

Light-Emitting Diode (LED): forward-biased junction where electron-hole recombination across the gap releases photons of energy $h\nu \approx E_g$. Choice of semiconductor (GaAs IR, GaP green/red, GaN blue) sets colour. Highly efficient, long life.

Solar cell: large-area p-n junction with no external bias; sunlight creates electron-hole pairs, the built-in field drives them to opposite electrodes — generating EMF (~0.5 V per cell). Used in calculators, satellites, power generation.

summary: Solids classified by band structure: metals (overlapping bands), semiconductors ($E_g \sim 1$ eV), insulators ($E_g > 3$ eV). Intrinsic semiconductors have $n_e = n_h$; doping creates n-type (donor, electrons majority) or p-type (acceptor, holes majority), with $n_e n_h = n_i^2$. p-n junction has depletion region and barrier $V_0$. Diodes conduct under forward bias, block under reverse — basis of rectifiers. Special diodes: Zener (regulator), photodiode (sensor), LED (emitter), solar cell (generator).
pitfalls: Holes are NOT real particles — they're vacancies, but behave as positive charges in an electric field.
n-type has electron majority but is electrically NEUTRAL (donor ions provide compensating + charge).
Mass-action law n_e × n_h = n_i² holds in BOTH n-type and p-type at equilibrium.
Depletion region has NO mobile carriers and a high resistance.
Forward-bias barrier ≈ 0.7 V for Si, 0.3 V for Ge (cut-in voltage).
Zener diode operates in REVERSE bias (in breakdown), not forward — it's a regulator there.
Half-wave rectifier output frequency = input frequency; full-wave = 2× input frequency.
